description: See MGCR 628J1 for course description.

name: Integrative Course
subject: MGCR
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MGCR 628J2
description: See MGCR 628J1 for course description.

name: Healthcare Leadership
subject: MGCR
description:
An introduction to the leadership challenges of the 21st century in a rapidly changing
global environment at the intersection of business and society.

name: Marketing Management
subject: MGCR
restrictions: Open only to M.B.A. students.
description: A comprehensive overview of the fundamental principles of marketing. The primary focus is on understanding customers and creating value by offering solutions to their problems that can be branded, communicated and distributed using an omnichannel approach. Appreciate the importance of marketing for business, marketing and ESG adoption and for designing winning marketing plans for organizations.

name: Managing Organizational Behaviour
subject: MGCR
restrictions: Open only to M.B.A. students.
description:
Exploration of social processes that affect human behaviour and decision making, and
tactics for building and managing organizations. Different organizational designs for optimizing different types of innovation. The intersection of organizational behaviour and sustainability, covering environmental but also social aspects of the latter. The ethics of management, including the governance of AI. The role of culture in
organizations and the management of organizational culture.

name: Accounting and Financial Reporting
subject: MGCR
restrictions: Open only to M.B.A. students.
description: The use of financial reporting including concepts and techniques in developing and interpreting accounting information. Identification of information that is relevant and useful for stakeholder decision making and analysis. Governance and ethical issues relating to the framework, accuracy and integrity of how this information is prepared and presented.

name: Elements of Modern Finance 1
subject: MGCR
description: Topics: appropriate evaluation criteria for projects, risk and return; how to construct efficient portfolios; rigorous techniques for valuing financial assets. Corporate financing strategies, efficient market theories and investment banking; principles of debt financing and Modigliani-Miller propositions.

name: Financial Reporting
subject: MGCR
restrictions: Open only to M.B.A. students.
description:
Basics of investments, including stocks, bonds and portfolio analysis, and the management of a company’s financial resources. How discounting and capital budgeting techniques help managers decide which projects should receive funding.
The focus will be on Net Present Value analysis and its limitations. How financial indicators are not always sufficient and do not incorporate considerations such as sustainability and the need to act ethically.

name: Business Tools
subject: MGCR
description: An introduction to the practical usage of standard business tools and frameworks.

name: Managing Resources
subject: MGCR
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MGCR 650
description: An introduction to the challenges of acquiring and developing various resources in the firm, including financial capital, human talent, and information technology.

name: Managing Resources
subject: MGCR
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MGCR 650
description: An introduction to the challenges of acquiring and developing various resources in the firm, including financial capital, human talent, and information technology.

name: Managing Resources
subject: MGCR
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MGCR 651D1
description: See MGCR 651D1 for description.

name: Value Creation
subject: MGCR
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MGCR 650.
description: Offers a comprehensive overview of the value creation process in business sectors by exploring the inter-relationships among the partners in the value chain.

name: Value Creation
subject: MGCR
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MGCR 650.
description: Offers a comprehensive overview of the value creation process in business sectors by exploring the inter-relationships among the partners in the value chain.

name: Value Creation
subject: MGCR
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MGCR 652D1.
description: See MGCR 652D1 for course description.

name: Markets and Globalization
subject: MGCR
description: A review of macro-environment in which firms compete, and the linkages which exist between the domestic and global economies. Topics such as trade, fiscal and monetary policy, interest rates, exchange rates, and balance of payments will be covered.

name: International Study Trip
subject: MGCR
description: This course aims to provide students a comprehensive understanding of the process of internationalization as well as managing an enterprise/organization in an international setting. Students will participate in an organized study trip outside of Canada, to a city with significant international business.

name: International Study Trip
subject: MGCR
description: This course aims to provide students a comprehensive understanding of the process of internationalization as well as managing an enterprise/organization in an international setting. Students will participate in an organized study trip outside of Canada, to a city with significant international business.

name: International Study Trip
subject: MGCR
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MGCR 660D1
description: This course aims to provide students a comprehensive understanding of the process of internationalization as well as managing an enterprise/organization in an international setting. Students will participate in an organized study trip outside of Canada, to a city with significant international business.

name: International Study Experience
subject: MGCR
description: The process of internationalization as well as managing an enterprise/organization in an international setting. Students will participate in an organized study trip outside of Canada, to a city with significant international business.

name: Introductory Microbiology
subject: MIMM
description: A general treatment of microbiology bearing specifically on the biological properties of microorganisms. Emphasis will be on procaryotic cells. Basic principles of microbial genetics are also introduced.

name: Laboratory in Microbiology
subject: MIMM
description: This laboratory course is designed to complement MIMM 211. Sessions introduce general techniques peculiar to the handling of microorganisms.

name: Introductory Immunology: Elements of Immunity
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: BIOL 200
description: Basic immunology, organs and cells, elements of innate immunity, phagocytes, complement, elements of adaptive immunity, B-cells, T-cells, antigen presenting cells, MHC genes and molecules, antigen processing and presentation, cytokines and chemokines. Emphasis on anatomy and the molecular and cellular players working together as a physiological system to maintain human health.

name: Scientific Writing Skills in MIMM
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM323, MIMM324, MIMM384
description: Scientific and communication skills, including searching the literature, reading primary papers, managing citations and using them appropriately, as well as interpreting scientific data, synthesizing findings from the scientific literature, and writing a scientific paper. This course will allow students to practice scientific writing skills through various steps intended to provide feedback.

name: Intermediate Immunology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MIMM 214
description: An intermediate-level immunology course covering the cellular and molecular basis of lymphocyte development and activation in immune responses in health and disease.

name: Microbial Physiology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MIMM 211
description: An introduction to the composition and structure of microbial cells, the biochemical activities associated with cellular metabolism and how these activities are regulated and coordinated. The course will have a molecular and genetic approach to the study of microbial physiology.

name: Fundamental Virology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM 211, BIOL 200, BIOL 201 or BIOC 212
description: A study of the fundamental properties of viruses and their interactions with host cells. Bacteriophages, DNA- and RNA-containing animal viruses, and retroviruses are covered. Emphasis will be on phenomena occurring at the molecular level and on the regulated control of gene expression in virus-infected cells.

name: Molecular Microbiology Laboratory
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): MIMM 211 and MIMM 212
restrictions: Not open to students who are taking or have taken MIMM 386D1/D2.
description: A series of laboratory exercises in molecular biology and microbial genetics during the Fall term. As part of a term paper writing project, students will explore contemporary research subjects that represent the wide interests of the Microbiology and Immunology departmentâ•Žs faculty. The general objective is to provide practical and theoretical introduction to the technology and methodology used in contemporary microbiology research.

name: Laboratory in Immunology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): MIMM 211, MIMM 212, MIMM 214 (MIMM 384 highly recommended).
description: This laboratory course allows students to gain expertise in following protocols and performing key techniques in immunology. Students also develop basic experimental design and scientific thinking skills by designing their own experiments based on available reagents to solve a problem. Students learn to keep a lab book and communicate scientific findings in written and oral form.

name: The Business of Science
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: At least one 200-level course in a biological or biomedical discipline or permission of instructor
description: The ability to select and manipulate genetic material has led to unprecedented interest in the industrial applications of procaryotic and eucaryotic cells. Beginning in the 1970s the introduction of and subsequent refinements to recombinant DNA technology and hybridoma technology transformed the horizons of the biopharmaceutical world. This course will highlight the important events that link basic research to clinical/commercial application of new drugs and chemicals.

name: SEA-PHAGES: Phage Discovery
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM 211, MIMM 212, BIOL 200
restrictions: Open to Microbiology and Immunology U2 and U3 students.
description: Microbiology and molecular biology content about bacteriophages, followed by hands-on experience through isolation and characterization of novel bacteriophages from soil samples using various microbiology and virology techniques.

name: SEA-PHAGES: Genome Annotation
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM 211, MIMM 212, BIOL 200
restrictions: Open to Microbiology and Immunology U2 and U3 students.
description: Introduction to bioinformatics as a tool to further bacteriophage research by using computer programs and software to annotate genomes of bacteriophages.

name: Undergraduate Research Project in Microbiology
subject: MIMM
restrictions: This course cannot be taken under the S/U option. Departmental permission required. Students cannot be supervised by the same instructor for two 396 Science courses. Open to students in programs offered by the Faculty of Science only.
description: Independent research project with a final written report.

name: Undergraduate Research Project in Immunology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: Departmental permission required.
restrictions: Open to students in programs offered by the Faculty of Science.
description: Independent research project with a final written report.

name: Parasitology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MIMM 314 or equivalent - ANAT 261 is strongly recommended
description: A study of the biology, immunological aspects of host-parasite interactions, pathogenicity, epidemiology and molecular biological aspects of selected parasites of medical importance. Laboratory will consist of a lecture on techniques, demonstrations and practical work.

name: Advanced Immunology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MIMM 314
description: An advanced course serving as a logical extension of MIMM 314. The course will integrate molecular, cellular and biochemical events involved in the ontogeny of the lymphoid system and its activation in the immune response. The course will provide the student with an up-to-date understanding of a rapidly moving field.

name: Bacterial Pathogenesis
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM 214, MIMM 323, MIMM 384.
description: Familiarizes students with key principles and recent advances in our understanding of the strategies that bacteria use to infect and cause disease, as well as the approaches used in the prevention and treatment of bacterial infections.

name: Viral Pathogenesis
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM 211, MIMM 324, MIMM 314
description: A study of the biological and molecular aspects of viral pathogenesis with emphasis on the human pathogenic viruses including the retroviruses HIV and HTLV-1; herpes viruses; papilloma viruses; hepatitis viruses; and new emerging human viral diseases. These viruses will be discussed in terms of virus multiplication, gene expression virus-induced cytopathic effects and host immune response to infection.

name: Microbiology Advanced Research Project
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): MIMM 212, MIMM 384, MIMM 385
description: This two-semester course is intended for undergraduate Science students who want a rigorous research experience beyond that of a one semester course (MIMM 396/397) and who are unlikely to take the Honours Research Project course (MIMM 501/502).

name:
Microbiology Advanced
 Research Project
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): MIMM 496D1, MIMM 212, MIMM 384, MIMM 385
description: For description see MIMM 496D1.

name: Immunology Advanced Research Project
subject: MIMM
description: This two-semester course is intended for undergraduate Science students who want a rigorous research experience beyond that of a one semester course (MIMM 396/397) and who are unlikely to take the Honours Research Project course (MIMM 501/502).

name: Immunology Advanced Research Project
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM 212, MIMM 384, MIMM 385
description: This two-semester course is intended for undergraduate Science students who want a rigorous research experience beyond that of a one semester course (MIMM 396/397) and who are unlikely to take the Honours Research Project course (MIMM 501/502).

name: Immunology Advanced Research Project
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM 497D1, MIMM 212, MIMM 384, MIMM 385
description: See MIMM 497D1 for description.

name: Library Research Project in Immunology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): MIMM 314, MIMM 323, MIMM 324 and MIMM 386.
restrictions: This course is intended for final year Microbiology and Immunology students only.
description: Supervised exploration of the current scientific literature on an assigned topic of an advanced nature within the  area of Immunology.

name: Library Research Project in Microbiology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MIMM 314, MIMM 323, MIMM 324 and MIMM 386.
restrictions: Open only to final year Microbiology and Immunology students.
description: Supervised exploration of the current scientific literature on an assigned topic of an advanced nature within the general areas of bacteriology, virology, or parasitology.

name: Honours Research Project in Immunology
subject: MIMM
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking MIMM 502D1/D2. Open to U3 Honours students and Majors students.
description: Presentation of students' research findings in a seminar and a final written report.

name: Honours Research Project in Immunology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MIMM 501D1
description: See MIMM 501D1 for course description.

name: Honours Research Project in Microbiology
subject: MIMM
restrictions: U3 Honours students and Majors students are eligible. Required CGPA: 3.50 or higher.
description: Presentation of students' research findings in a seminar and a final written report.

name: Honours Research Project in Microbiology
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MIMM 502D1
description: See MIMM 502D1 for course description.

name: Inflammatory Processes
subject: MIMM
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MIMM 314
description: This course concentrates on the non-specific aspects of the immune response, an area which is not adequately covered by the other immunology courses presented at the university. Interactions between guest researchers (from McGill and other universities) and students will be furthered.

name: New Advances in Antimicrobial Drugs
subject: MIMM
description: Overview on bacteria, insights into bacterial pathogenesis, an understanding of antibiotics, and new developments in antibacterial to beat antibiotic resistance.

name: Biochemical Pathology
subject: MIMM
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking PATH 607.
description: Immunopathogenesis of Human Disease: Lectures and seminars on immune-regulatory mechanisms (cellular/molecular), maintaining the balance between protection and pathology; pathogenesis of major infectious diseases with bacterial, viral and parasitic involvement (e.g. tuberculosis, AIDS, malaria) and classical immune disorders (e.g. asthma).

name: Graduate Seminars 1
subject: MIMM
description: Presentation of a research proposal seminar, which is not held publically but presented and evaluated in the context of the first Advisory Committee meeting.  Presentation of the relevant background of the research project, the hypothesis to be tested, the objectives of the research project and summary of the experimental approach that will be used.

name: Graduate Seminars 2
subject: MIMM
restrictions: M.Sc. students - presentation of two seminar topics throughout the course of their degree program
description: Presentation of a scientific seminar, which includes a summary of the relevant background of the research project, the hypothesis being tested, the student’s experimental work to date and the future objectives.

name: Current Topics 1
subject: MIMM
description: Graduate students are required to attend eight scientific seminars each term, for three terms. A minimum of four seminars should be attended from the Infection and Immunity Seminar Series offered by the Department of Microbiology and Immunology. A maximum of four life sciences-related seminars presented by principal investigators can be attended at McGill University or its affiliated centers (MUHC, LDI, IRCM).

name: Current Topics 2
subject: MIMM
description: Graduate students are required to attend eight scientific seminars each term, for three terms. A minimum of four seminars should be attended from the Infection and Immunity Seminar Series offered by the Department of Microbiology and Immunology. A maximum of four life sciences-related seminars presented by principal investigators can be attended at McGill University or its affiliated centers (MUHC, LDI, IRCM).

name: Current Topics 3
subject: MIMM
description: M.Sc. Students (discussion groups with guest speakers).

name: Reading and Conference 1
subject: MIMM
restrictions: M.Sc. students - two of these courses required throughout the course of their degree program
description: Student presentations, taken from current literature, are concerned with aspects of a central topic. Presentations are designed to be informal and to generate student discussions. Topic will change from term to term.

name: Reading and Conference 2
subject: MIMM
restrictions: M.Sc. students - two of these courses required throughout the course of their degree program
description: Student presentations, taken from current literature, are concerned with aspects of a central topic. Presentations are designed to be informal and to generate student discussions. Topic will change from term to term.

name: Reading and Conference 4
subject: MIMM
restrictions: M.Sc. students - two of these courses required throughout the course of their degree program
description: Student presentations, taken from current literature, are concerned with aspects of a central topic. Presentations are designed to be informal and to generate student discussions. Topic will change from term to term.

name: Master's Research 1
subject: MIMM
restrictions: M.Sc. students
description: Independent work under the direction of a supervisor on a research problem in the student's designated area of research.

name: Master's Research 2
subject: MIMM
restrictions: M.Sc. students
description: Independent work under the direction of a supervisor on a research problem in the student's designated area of research.

name: Master's Research 3
subject: MIMM
restrictions: M.Sc. students
description: Independent work under the direction of a supervisor on a research problem in the student's designated area of research.

name: Graduate Seminars 3
subject: MIMM
restrictions: Ph.D. students
description: Presentation of a maximum of three seminars topics throughout the course of their degree program.

name: Introduction to Neuroscience 1
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: BIOL 112, CHEM 110, CHEM 120, PHYS 101 or PHYS 131, and PHYS 102 or PHYS 142. Pre-/Co-requisite BIOL 200, CHEM 212 or permission of instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who are taking or have taken PHGY 209.
description: An introduction to how nerve cells generate action potentials, communicate with one another at synapses, develop synaptic connections, early brain development, and the construction of specific neural circuits.

name: Introduction to Neuroscience 2
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: NSCI 200 or PSYC 211 or permission of instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PSYC 308.
description: An introduction to how the nervous system acquires and integrates information and uses it to produce behaviour.

name: Neuroethics
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: NSCI 200 and NSCI 201 or permission of instructor
restrictions: Open to students in the Major Neuroscience Program
description: An introduction to ethical issues arising from basic and clinical neuroscience. Overview of therapeutic, diagnostic, and research interventions in mental and neurological disorders, and their implications on society.

name: Undergraduate Research Project
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: At least one term of undergraduate studies, a CGPA of at least 3.0, or permission of instructor to waive these requirements. A project proposal form must be completed by the student and instructor and approved by the unit head or his/her delegate before the start of the term. Instructors will list project-specific prerequisites with the project description.
restrictions: This course cannot be taken under the S/U option. Coordinator's permission required. Students cannot be supervised by the same instructor for two 396 Science courses. Open to students in programs offered by the Faculty of Science only.
description: Independent research project with a final written report.

name: Neuroscience Seminar
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: NSCI 200, 201, and 300
restrictions: Open to students in their final year of a B.Sc. Major Neuroscience Program
description: Analysis of current research in neuroscience.

name: Neuroscience Seminar
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: NSCI 400D1
description: See NSCI 400D1 for course description.

name: Independent Research 1
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: NSCI 200 and NSCI 201
restrictions: Only open to students registered in the B.Sc. Neuroscience Major. Not open to students who have taken or are taking NSCI 420D1 and NSCI 420D2.
description: Independent laboratory research in neuroscience.

name: Independent Research 1
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: NSCI 200 and NSCI 201
restrictions: Only open to students registered in the B.Sc. Neuroscience Major. Not open to students who have taken or are taking NSCI 420D1 and NSCI 420D2
description: Independent laboratory research in neuroscience.

name: Independent Research 1
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: NSCI 410D1
description: See NSCI 410D1 for course description.

name: Independent Research 2
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: NSCI 200 and NSCI 201
description: Independent laboratory research in neuroscience.

name: Independent Research 2
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: NSCI 200 and NSCI 201
restrictions: Only open to students registered in the B.Sc. Neuroscience Major. Not open to students who have taken or are taking NSCI 410.
description: Independent laboratory research in neuroscience.

name: Independent Research 2
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: NSCI 420D1
restrictions: Only open to students registered in the B.Sc. Neuroscience Major. Not open to students who have taken or are taking NSCI 410.
description: See NSCI 420D1 for course description.

name: Independent Research 2
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: NSCI 200 and NSCI 201
restrictions: Only open to students registered in the B.Sc. Neuroscience Major. Not open to students who have taken or are taking NSCI 410.
description: Independent laboratory research in neuroscience.

name: Independent Research 2
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: NSCI 420N1
description: See NSCI 420N1 for course description.

name: Honours Research Project
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: NSCI 200, NSCI 201, NSCI 300
restrictions: Only open to U3 students registered in the B.Sc. Honours Neuroscience. Requires departmental approval.
description: Students undertake an independent research project in the field of neursocience under the guidance of an established McGill neuroscientist. They are expected to obtain hands-on research experience in their supervisor's laboratory, learn about the strengths and limitations of the methodology and work independently.

name: Honours Research Project
subject: NSCI
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: NSCI 430D1
restrictions: Only open to U3 students registered in the B.Sc. Honours Neuroscience. Requires departmental approval.
description: Students undertake an independent research project in the field of neursocience under the guidance of an established McGill neuroscientist. They are expected to obtain hands-on research experience in their supervisor's laboratory, learn about the strengths and limitations of the methodology and work independently.

name: Cutting-Edge Research in Physiology
subject: PHGY
restrictions: Open to students registered in Core Liberal Physiology, Major Physiology and Honours Physiology programs.
description: An overview of physiology through an examination of current research topics and experimental approaches. Emphasis is placed on applications of physiology to modern medicine and careers in physiology.

name: Mammalian Physiology 1
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: BIOL 112, CHEM 110, CHEM 120, PHYS 101 or PHYS 131, and PHYS 102 or PHYS 142. Pre-/co-requisites: BIOL 200, CHEM 212 or equivalent.
restrictions: For students in the Faculty of Science, and other students by permission of the instructor
description: Physiology of body fluids, blood, body defense mechanisms, muscle, peripheral, central, and autonomic nervous systems.

name: Mammalian Physiology 2
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: BIOL 112, CHEM 110, CHEM 120, PHYS 101 or PHYS 131, and PHYS 102 or PHYS 142. Pre-/co-requisite: BIOL 200, BIOL 201, BIOC 212, CHEM 212 or equivalent.
restrictions: For students in the Faculty of Science, and other students by permission of the instructor
description: Physiology of cardiovascular, respiratory, digestive, endocrine and renal systems.

name: Introductory Physiology Laboratory 1
subject: PHGY
restrictions: Required for Physiology students enrolled in PHGY 209. Open to BA &Sc. students and to others by permission of the instructor. Not open to students who have taken PHGY 212D1/D2.
description: Exercises illustrating fundamental principles in physiology: Biological Signals Acquisitions, Blood, Immunology, Neurophysiology, Neuromuscular Physiology.

name: Introductory Physiology Laboratory 2
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 212
restrictions: Required for Physiology students enrolled in PHGY 210. Open to BA &Sc. students and to others by permission of the instructor. Not open to students who have taken PHGY 212D1/D2.
description: Exercises illustrating fundamental principles in physiology: Central Nervous System, Cardiovascular, Respiration, Exercise Physiology, Molecular Endocrinology.

name: Channels, Synapses and Hormones
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 209 or permission of the instructor.
description: In-depth presentation of experimental results and hypotheses on cellular communication in the nervous system and the endocrine system.

name: Respiratory, Renal, and Cardiovascular Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 209 and PHGY 210 or equivalent, PHGY 311 or permission of the instructor
description: In-depth presentation of experimental results and hypotheses underlying our current understanding of topics in renal, respiratory and cardiovascular functions explored beyond the introductory level.

name: Blood, Gastrointestinal, and Immune Systems Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 209 and PHGY 210 or equivalent, PHGY 311 or permission of the instructor
description: In-depth presentation of experimental results and hypotheses underlying our current understanding of topics in immunology, blood and fluids, and gastrointestinal physiology.

name: Integrative Neuroscience
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 209
description: In depth presentation of experimental results and hypotheses underlying our current understanding of how single neurons and ensembles of neurons encode sensory information, generate movement, and control cognitive functions such as emotion, learning, and memory, during voluntary behaviours.

name: Research Techniques: Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 209, PHGY 210 and PHGY 311.
restrictions: Honours Physiology students
description: Provides an overview of common research methods in Physiology, including critical analysis and practical experience with some of the methods. Topics include research ethics of animal experimentation, data analysis, membrane biophysics, radioimmunoassay, ion sensitive dyes, immunocytochemistry, localization techniques, protein transport, cell sorting and molecular biology.

name: Tutorial in Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 209 and PHGY 210 or equivalent.
restrictions: Enrolment restricted to Honours Physiology students
description: The course consists of regularly scheduled meetings between each individual student and a chosen staff member, to consider current problems in biomedical research and to develop background for a research project to be carried out in U3. Brief written summaries of each meeting are required.

name: Tutorial in Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 359D1
description: See PHGY 359D1 for course description.

name: Undergraduate Research Project
subject: PHGY
restrictions: This course cannot be taken under the S/U option. Departmental permission required. Students cannot be supervised by the same instructor for two 396 Science courses. Open to students in programs offered by the Faculty of Science only.
description: Independent research project with a final written report.

name: Immunology Research Project
subject: PHGY
restrictions: Enrolment restricted to U3 Interdepartmental Honours Immunology students or permission of instructors
description: Individual research projects in immunology under the guidance of staff members in the three participating departments: Physiology, Biochemistry, and Microbiology and Immunology.

name: Immunology Research Project
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 419D1
restrictions: Enrolment restricted to U3 Interdepartmental Honours Immunology students or permission of instructors
description: See PHGY 419D1 for course description.

name: Analyzing Physiological Systems
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 311, PHGY 314, BIOL 200 or permission from instructor.
description: An introduction to quantitative analysis of physiological data, both to the mode of thinking and to a set of tools that allows accurate predictions of biological systems. Examples will range from oscillating genetic networks to understanding higher brain function. Modelling and data analysis through examples and exercises will be emphasized.

name: Advanced Neurophysiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 311 or equivalent
restrictions: Departmental approval required
description: Topics of current interest in neurophysiology including the development of neurons and synapses, physiology of ionic channels, presynaptic and postsynaptic events in synaptic transmission and neuronal interactions in CNS function.

name: Physiology Seminar
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: permission of instructors
description: Discussion of topics in mammalian, cellular and molecular physiology. Students will be required to write one essay and make at least one oral presentation per term. A final course essay is required.

name: Physiology Seminar
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 459D1
description: See PHGY 459D1 for course description.

name: Experimental Physiology
subject: PHGY
restrictions: Departmental approval required. This course is a requirement for U3 students in the Honours Physiology program, the Major Program in Physiology and Mathematics, and the Major program in Physiology and Physics, and is open to a limited number of other U3 Physiology students.
description: Individual project work under the supervision of Departmental Staff members.

name: Experimental Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 461D1
description: See PHGY 461D1 for course description.

name: Stem Cell Biology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): PHGY 313 or by permission of instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who are taking or have taken ANAT 416.
description: The main concepts in stem cell biology: embryonic stem cells, induced pluripotent stem cells, cancer stem cells, stem cells populations of many adult tissues, applications of stem cell biology and ethical issues surrounding stem cell use in research and medicine.  The major experimental methods and laboratory techniques in stem cell biology.

name: Independent Study in Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 311, PHGY 312, PHGY 313, PHGY 314. Note: A form from the Student Affairs Officer, Room 1022, McIntyre Medical Bldg., must be completed prior to registration.
restrictions: Open only to final year Physiology students
description: Supervised research project in physiology.

name: Exercise Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 311, PHGY 312, and PHGY 313
description: Behaviour of physiological processes in response to physical effort, in areas such as structural basis of muscle contraction, thermoregulation during exercise, mechanics and energetics of muscle contraction, fuel utilization, fatigue, physiological adjustments during exercise and influence of training.

name: Advanced Renal Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite (Undergraduate): PHGY 312 or the equivalent
restrictions: Open to advanced undergraduate and graduate students
description: Advanced concepts in selected areas of kidney physiology, including the glomerulus, renal cell biology, kidney development, membrane and epithelial transport, hormones and autacoids, kidney transplantation, bioengineering and regenerative medicine.

name: Translational Immunology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: MIMM 314 or PHGY 313 or permission of the instructor
description: Advanced key concepts in immunology as they relate to health and disease, including infectious diseases, non-infectious diseases and autoimmunity, and cancer immunology.

name: Blood-Brain Barrier in Health and Disease
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 313 or PHGY 314 and permission of the instructor
description: Molecular and cellular mechanisms underlying the organization and regulation of the blood-brain barrier, highlighting neurological diseases associated with the blood-brain barrier breakdown and give an overview of experimental approaches to study blood-brain barrier.

name: Physiology of Blood
subject: PHGY
description: Bone marrow hematopoiesis, with emphasis on regulation of stem cell proliferation and differentiation along hematopoietic pathways. Formation and differentiation of red and white blood cells and some of the diseases associated with hematopoiesis will be covered. Emphasis will be given to the molecular mechanisms involved in the normal and pathological conditions.

name: Artificial Cells
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite (Undergraduate): permission of instructors.
description: Physiology, biotechnology, chemistry and biomedical application of artificial cells, blood substitutes, immobilized enzymes, microorganisms and cells, hemoperfusion, artificial kidneys, and drug delivery systems. PHGY 517 and PHGY 518 when taken together, will give a complete picture of this field. However, the student can select one of these.

name: Ion Channels
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 311
description: A discussion of the principal theories and interesting new developments in the study of ion channels. Based on a textbook, computer exercises and critical reading and presentation of research papers. Topics include: Properties of voltage-and ligand-gated channels, single channel analysis, structure and function of ion channels.

name: Chronobiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 209 and PHGY 210 (or NSCI 200 and NSCI 201), and a relevant 300-level course (PHGY 311, or PHGY 314, or PSYC 318, or BIOC 311, or other, with permission of course coordinator).
restrictions: Course for senior undergraduate (U3) and graduate students
description:
An introduction to the field of chronobiology. The aim is to provide basic instruction on different
types of biological rhythms, with particular focus on circadian rhythms.

name: Cortical Plasticity
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: NSCI 200 or PHGY 314 or permission of the instructor
description: An examination of cortical plasticity following peripheral and central nervous damage from a systems/cognitive neuroscience perspective. Focus is on sensory systems and animal models, including the following topics: ocular dominance columns, retinal lesions, blindness, deafness, cochlear implants, barrel fields, amputation, stroke, and hemispherectomy. Experimental approaches to be considered include: electrophysiological recording, psychophysics, behaviour, and functional imaging.

name: Topics in Applied Immunology
subject: PHGY
restrictions: Permission of the instructor. U3 InterDept. Honours Immunology students and graduate students with strong immunology background i.e. PHGY 513 and BIOC 503
description: Seminar format course in which experts in immunologic mechanisms of resistance against a variety of infectious diseases, including AIDS, malaria, and tuberculosis oversee student moderators in their presentation of recent scientific literature in the field.

name: Molecular Physiology of Bone
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHGY 311, and BIOL 202 or equivalent
restrictions: U3 Physiology students, and graduate students in biomedical departments; others by permission of the instructor
description: Students will develop a working knowledge of cartilage and bone. Discussion topics will include: molecular and cellular environment of bone; heritable and acquired skeletal defects; research models used to study metabolic bone disease.

name: Cellular and Molecular Physiology
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHGY 311
description: Discussions of recent significant advances in our understanding of the gene products involved in diverse cellular signalling pathways. Topics will include cell-surface hormone receptors, nuclear steroid hormone receptors, and ion channels and transporters. Students will present and critically evaluate experimental approaches, results and interpretations of selected research publications.

name: Topics in Systems Neuroscience
subject: PHGY
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHGY 456
description: Topics of current interest in systems neurophysiology and behavioural neuroscience including: the neural representation of sensory information and motor behaviours, models of sensory motor integration, and the computational analysis of problems in motor control and perception. Students will be expected to present and critically discuss journal articles in class.

name: Light Microscopy-Life Science
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 or permission of instructors.
description: Introduction to optics, light microscopy imaging and data analysis for life scientists.

name: M.Sc. Proposal Seminar
subject: PHGY
description: Seminar presentation to Supervisory Committee and students on the Master's thesis proposal.

name: Literature Search and Research Proposal
subject: PHGY
description: Independent work under the supervision of the thesis advisor including literature search and research leading to thesis proposal.

name: Systems Biology and Biophysics
subject: PHGY
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: Knowledge of differential equations at the MATH 315 level or equivalent.
description: Introduction to classical and current topics in biophysics and systems biology in order to model the control of gene expression and intracellular signal transduction, as well as gene spread in populations.

name: Laboratory Research 1
subject: PHGY
description: Laboratory research leading to the thesis.

name: Laboratory Research 2
subject: PHGY
description: Laboratory research leading to the thesis.

name: Progress in Research
subject: PHGY
description: Progress in research in preparation of thesis.

name: Thesis 1
subject: PHGY
description: Written and oral presentation of thesis proposal to the research Supervisory Committee.

name: Thesis 2
subject: PHGY
description: Written and oral preparation of the thesis.

name: M.Sc. Final Seminar
subject: PHGY
description: Final seminar presentation to students supervisory committee prior to thesis submission.

name: Ph.D. Progress Seminar 1
subject: PHGY
description: The Progress Seminar is a "work in progress" seminar on what the student has accomplished to date. Following completion of the comprehensive exam, the seminar should be presented to the student's supervisory committee as a formal presentation of approximately 30 minutes followed by a question and discussion period.

name: Ph.D. Progress Seminar 2
subject: PHGY
description: A "work in progress" seminar, intended as a report on student progress following the Thesis Proposal Seminar.

name: Ph.D. Seminar Course 1
subject: PHGY
description: Required for Ph.D. students. Coordinated in conjunction with the weekly Departmental seminar series, students will meet for one hour before each seminar to critically discuss papers on the subject of the weekly seminar. Students will take turns introducing the papers and leading discussions on an overview of the research topic, some of the methodologies, results and conclusions.

name: Ph.D. Seminar Course 2
subject: PHGY
description: Required for Ph.D. students. Coordinated in conjunction with the weekly Departmental seminar series, students will meet for one hour before each seminar to critically discuss papers on the subject of the weekly seminar. Students will take turns introducing the papers and leading discussions on an overview of the research topic, some of the methodologies, results and conclusions.

Students will take turns introducing the papers and leading discussions on an overview of the research topic, some of the methodologies, results and conclusions.

subject: PHGY
description: Required for Ph.D. students. Students will take turns introducing the papers and leading discussions on an overview of the research topic, some of the methodologies, results and conclusions.

name: FYS: Right and Wrong
subject: PHIL
restrictions: Open only to newly admitted students in U0 or U1 who may take only one FYS
description: Reading and discussion of classic and contemporary philosophical texts on morality: its nature, status, and importance.

name: FYS: Knowledge and Ideas in Early Modern Philosophy
subject: PHIL
restrictions: Open only to newly admitted students in U0 or U1 who may take only one FYS
description: An introduction to central issues in the philosophy of the early modern period through an examination of works by, for example, Descartes, Malebranche, Spinoza, Locke, Leibniz, Berkeley and Hume.

name: FYS: Minds, Brain, and Machines
subject: PHIL
restrictions: Open only to newly admitted students in U0 or U1 who may take only one FYS
description: An introduction to the philosophical foundations of the sciences of the mind.

name: Introduction to Philosophy 1
subject: PHIL
description: A course treating some of the central problems of philosophy: the mind-body problem, freedom, scepticism and certainty, fate, time, and the existence of God.

name: Introduction to Philosophy 2
subject: PHIL
description: An introduction to some of the major problems of philosophy. This course does not duplicate PHIL 200.

name: The Good Life
subject: PHIL
description: Explores how philosophers from Socrates to Sartre wrestle with the question of how we should live, including ancient philosophers who present their teachings as pathways to happiness, virtue, and peace of mind; medieval philosophers who seek to understand God’s will; and modern philosophers who grapple with the tension between community and freedom. Analyzes debates of fundamental moral and existential questions: What things really matter? Does it pay to be good? What constitutes the best society? Can we escape the roller coaster of our emotions? Examines how answers are intertwined with beliefs about God, nature, knowledge, the soul, and politics.

name: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence Ethics
subject: PHIL
description: An introduction to several important debates in the ethics of artificial intelligence (AI), including: artificial general intelligence, artificial consciousness, existential risk, and the moral status of advanced AI systems, to practical ethical and political issues raised by the uses of AI systems, including opacity, discrimination, privacy,disinformation and impacts on work and wealth distribution.

name: Introduction to Deductive Logic 1
subject: PHIL
restrictions: Not open to students who are taking or have taken MATH 318
description: An introduction to propositional and predicate logic; formalization of arguments, truth tables, systems of deduction, elementary metaresults, and related topics.

name: Introduction to History and Philosophy of Science 2
subject: PHIL
description: A survey of the development of modern science since the Eighteenth Century.

name: Introduction to Moral Philosophy 1
subject: PHIL
description: A survey of a number of historically important and influential theories. Philosophers to be discussed may include Aristotle, Hume, Kant, Bentham, Mill, and Moore.

name: Contemporary Moral Issues
subject: PHIL
description: An introductory discussion of central ethical questions (the value of persons, or the relationship of rights and utilities, for example) through the investigation of currently disputed social and political issues. Specific issues to be discussed may include pornography and censorship, affirmative action, civil disobedience, punishment, abortion, and euthanasia.

name: Political Philosophy 1
subject: PHIL
description: An introduction to contemporary philosophy of politics by concentrating on a number of contested concepts, such as freedom, justice and equality, in contemporary political philosophy and practice.

name: Introduction to Feminist Theory
subject: PHIL
description: An introduction to feminist theory as political theory. Emphasis is placed on the plurality of analyses and proposals that constitute contemporary feminist thought. Some of the following are considered: liberal feminism, marxist and socialist feminism, radical feminism, postmodern feminism, francophone feminism, and the contributions to feminist theory by women of colour and lesbians.

name: Philosophical Fundamentals
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: two previous courses in philosophy, one of which must be PHIL 210 or written consent of the Department
restrictions: Open only to and required of Philosophy Honours and Joint Honours students
description: An intensive study of basic philosophical skills; reading, writing, analysis, and argumentation.

name: Philosophy of Mind
subject: PHIL
description: A survey of major positions of the mind-body problem, focusing on such questions as: Do we have minds and bodies? Can minds affect bodies? Is mind identical to body? If so, in what sense "identical"? Can physical bodies be conscious.

name: Intermediate Logic
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 210 or equivalent
description: A second course in Logic. NB. The course will be technical in nature, and some mathematical aptitude is essential. The emphasis is on the expressive properties of standard logical systems, including implications for the philosophy of mathematics. We will study the Completeness of First-Order Logic, then the 'limitative' theorems of Tarski and Gödel.

name: Philosophy of Mathematics
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 210
description: This course provides an historically informed introduction to philosophy of mathematics. It gives the student an overview of prominent issues and arguments, to enable her to follow and discuss contemporary research in philosophy of mathematics.

name: Philosophy of Race
subject: PHIL
description: An introduction to issues in the philosophy of race, for example: the metaphysical status of race; the biology of racial categories; the social construction of race; the relationship between race and racism; the phenomenology of racialized subjectivity; or, intersections of race, gender, and other identity categories.

name: Ethical Theory
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: one of PHIL 230, PHIL 237, PHIL 242, PHIL 343, or written permission of the instructor
description: A course focusing on central questions in ethical theory such as the nature of the good and the right and the factors which determine moral rightness and wrongness.

name: Aesthetics
subject: PHIL
description: An introduction to issues central to aesthetic theory; the nature of aesthetic judgment, perception of the aesthetic object, the nature of the art object.

name: Philosophy of Science 1
subject: PHIL
description: A discussion of philosophical problems as they arise in the context of scientific practice and enquiry. Such issues as the philosophical presuppositions of the physical and social sciences, the nature of scientific method and its epistemological implications will be addressed.

name: Biomedical Ethics
subject: PHIL
description: An investigation of ethical issues as they arise in the practice of medicine (informed consent, e.g.) or in the application of medical technology (in vitro fertilization, euthanasia, e.g.)

name: Medieval and Renaissance Political Theory
subject: PHIL
description: A study of Medieval and Renaissance political theory.

name: Greek Political Theory
subject: PHIL
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken POLI 333
description: An examination of the ethical and political theories of ancient Greece, especially those of Plato and Aristotle.

name: Philosophy of Law 1
subject: PHIL
restrictions: This course is intended for students with a non-professional interest in law, as well as for those considering law as a profession
description: A discussion of the nature of justice and law, and of the relationship between them.

name: Environmental Philosophy
subject: PHIL
description: An introduction to environmental ethics; potentially also such related fields as philosophy of ecology, environmental aesthetics, and/or other philosophical topics demonstrably relevant to environmental issues.

name: History and Philosophy of Ancient Science
subject: PHIL
description: Topics in ancient pure mathematics (geometry and number theory), "mixed mathematics" (astronomy, music theory, optics, mechanics), and/or natural science (including medicine), studied with a view to philosophical issues raised by the content of ancient science and/or by the logic of scientific argument.

name: The Presocratic Philosophers
subject: PHIL
description: An examination of the surviving fragments of the presocratic philosophers and schools of philosophy, as well as later reports of their views.

name: Plato
subject: PHIL
description: An examination of some of the philosophical problems (those in logic, epistemology, metaphysics, and ethics, e.g.) found in a selection of Plato's dialogues.

name: Aristotle
subject: PHIL
description: An examination of selected works by Aristotle. The course considers issues in moral philosophy as well as those found in the logical treatises, the Physics and Metaphysics, and in the philosophy of mind.

name: Early Medieval Philosophy
subject: PHIL
description: An examination of selected works in the Christian, Islamic and Jewish traditions. Topics in moral and political philosophy, logic and metaphysics, philosophical psychology and epistemology, philosophy of science, and philosophical theology may be discussed.

name: 17th Century Philosophy
subject: PHIL
description: An examination of the work of such seventeenth-century philosophers as Descartes, Hobbes, Gassendi, Malebranche, Leibniz, and the Cambridge Platonists.

name: 18th Century Philosophy
subject: PHIL
description: A survey of eighteenth century philosophy, especially British philosophy. Attention is given to fundamental metaphysical, epistemological, and moral issues as reflected in the work of such philosophers as Locke, Shaftesbury, Berkeley, Hutcheson, Butler, Hume and Reid.

name: 18th and Early 19th Century German Philosophy
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 360 or PHIL 361 is recommended
description: An examination of the works of such philosophers as Kant, Fichte, Jacobi, Schelling, and Hegel.

name: 19th Century Philosophy
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: A previous course in philosophy is recommended
description: An examination of the works of such 19th century philosophers as Mach, Helmholtz, Dedekind, Frege, Marx, Kierkegaard, Schopenhauer, Nietzsche, Mill and Bradley.

name: Existentialism
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: one course in philosophy
description: This course will examine the nature of existentialist thought as represented in various philosophical and literary texts. Particular themes to be examined include freedom, alienation, responsibility and choice, and the nature of self.

name: Tutorial 01
subject: PHIL
restrictions: Open to second year Full Honours students in Philosophy and to other students, with consent of the Department
description: Individualized guided research on an approved topic in philosophy.

name: Tutorial 02
subject: PHIL
restrictions: Open to second year Full Honours students in Philosophy and to other students, with consent of the Department
description: Individualized guided research on an approved topic in philosophy.

name: Topics in Philosophy of Logic and Mathematics
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHIL 210 or the equivalent, and one intermediate course in philosophy
description: A course focusing on some philosophical issue (e.g., the nature of numbers or the relation of truth to provability) as it arises in the study of mathematics and logic.

name: Philosophy of Language
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHIL 210 or equivalent and one intermediate course in philosophy
description: An examination of central notions in the philosophy of language (reference, meaning, and truth, e.g.), the puzzles these notions give rise to, and the relevance of these notions to such questions as: What is language? How is communication possible? What is understanding? Is language rule-governed.

name: Epistemology
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 210 or equivalent and one intermediate course in philosophy
description: A discussion of central topics in the theory of knowledge. The questions addressed in the course may include: What is knowledge? Do we have any knowledge? What is the relation between knowledge and belief? When is belief justified? Is all knowledge conscious knowledge.

name: Metaphysics
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHIL 210 or equivalent and one intermediate course in philosophy
description: An examination of central questions in metaphysics in their historical and contemporary forms. Topics may be chosen from such issues as: personal identity, the nature of space and time, the nature of events and properties, possible worlds, and the problem of realism.

name: Topics in Critical Philosophy of Race
subject: PHIL
description: An advanced discussion of topics in the critical philosophy of race.

name: Metaethics
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 334 or written permission of the instructor
description: A study of the status of sentences containing moral terms, judgements about moral claims, and the nature of moral facts.

name: Aesthetics 2
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 336 or written permission of the instructor
description: An advanced discussion of issues in aesthetics.

name: Philosophy of Science 2
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 341 or written permission of the instructor
description: An analysis of some key philosophical ideas in science and technology, e.g. problem, explanation, forecast, testability and truth.

name: Topics in Feminist Theory
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 242 and one intermediate course in philosophy
description: Advanced discussion of topical and central themes in feminist theory.

name: Early Modern Political Theory
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: at least one course in political philosophy
description: A survey of political and moral theory from the Reformation to the French Revolution including Luther, Montaigne, Descartes, Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau and Smith.

name: 19th Century Political Theory
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: at least one course in political philosophy
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken POLI 434
description: An examination of various strands of political theory since Rousseau, concentrating on such themes as the understanding of modernity and theories of liberal society.

name: Current Issues in Political Philosophy
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: at least one course in political philosophy
description: Selected issues in contemporary political philosophy.

name: Major Philosophers 1
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: one intermediate course in philosophy
description: This seminar will give detailed attention to the work of one philosopher or to a single philosophical theme addressed by several philosophers. Emphasis will be placed on understanding how the metaphysical, epistemological, and moral views of a figure or figures are internally related. Topic will vary from year to year.

name: Later Greek Philosophy
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 354 or PHIL 355
description: An examination of some of the major post-Aristotelian schools of philosophy. Texts from the Peripatetic, Stoic, Epicurean, Sceptical, Platonic, and medical traditions may be considered. Problems in logic, ethics, physics, epistemology, and metaphysics will be addressed.

name: Ancient Metaphysics and Natural Philosophy
subject: PHIL
description: An examination of central themes of ancient metaphysics and/or natural philosophy as treated by two or more contrasting philosophers or philosophical traditions - probably including Plato and/or Aristotle, and possibly including some Hellenistic or post-Hellenistic schools.

name: Ancient Moral Theory
subject: PHIL
description: An examination of central themes of ancient moral theory as treated by two or more contrasting philosophers or philosophical traditions - probably including Plato and/or Aristotle, and possibly some Hellenistic or post-Hellenistic schools.

name: Major Philosophers 2
subject: PHIL
description: This seminar will give detailed attention to the work of one philosopher or to a single philosophical theme addressed by several philosophers. Emphasis will be placed on understanding how the metaphysical, epistemological, and moral views of a figure or figures are internally related.

name: Topics in Contemporary Analytic Philosophy
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 370, PHIL 415 or written permission of instructor
description: An advanced discussion of major themes in the analytic tradition.

name: Phenomenology
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: one intermediate course in philosophy
description: A study of phenomenology from a historical and thematic perspective. The course will typically involve the study of central thinkers such as Husserl, Heidegger, or Merleau-Ponty, with an examination of the nature and development of the phenomenological movement.

name: Topics in Contemporary European Philosophy
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: one intermediate course in philosophy
description: Advanced discussion of selected themes in contemporary European philosophy.

name: Topics in the History of Philosophy
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: one intermediate course in philosophy
description: An advanced discussion of some theme and/or problem in the history of philosophy.

name: Topics in Philosophy
subject: PHIL
description: Topics in philosophy.  Topic varies by year.

restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 506.

subject: PHIL

restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 536.

subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 541.

name: Seminar: Feminist Theory
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): Any 400-level, 3-credit course in political philosophy, or permission of the instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 542.
description: An advanced course devoted to a specific topic in feminist theory: e.g., a major figure; or theme, such as sex/gender, embodiment, race, subjectivity, agency, representation, politics, nature/culture, discourse and power; or a feminist approach to the history of philosophy, ethics, social/political philosophy, epistemology, philosophy of science, phenomenology, or metaphysics.

name: Seminar: Medical Ethics
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 543.
description: An advanced course devoted to a particular philosophical problem as it arises in the context of medical practice or the application of medical technology.

restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 551.

subject: PHIL

restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 560.

subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.

subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): Permission of the instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 570.
description: An advanced course on some major analytic philosopher, or some issue of central importance in the analytic tradition.

name: Seminar: Contemporary European Philosophy
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 575.

subject: PHIL
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHIL 580.

description: A series of seminars on selected topics designed to provide professional training to graduate students. Topics will be selected from the general area of Metaphysics/Epistemology.

name: Fundamentals of Logic
subject: PHIL
description: A course in intermediate logic for graduate students in Philosophy, covering such topics as axiomatic systems, formal semantics, consistency, completeness, the limitative results, intuitionistic logic, formal theories of truth, aspects of the development of logic.

name: Teaching Reflection and Practice
subject: PHIL
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHIL 702
restrictions: Graduate students in philosophy
description:
Course design, development, delivery, and evaluation of a course that the student has been assigned to teach. Aspects of course design and in-class student
evaluations will be provided by a Faculty mentor.

name: Guided Research: Philosophy of Politics 2
subject: PHIL
description: Guided research on a theme and/or problem in the philosophy of politics.

name: Guided Research: Epistemology/Metaphysics 1
subject: PHIL
description: Guided research on a theme and/or problem in epistemology/metaphysics.

name: Guided Research: Epistemology/Metaphysics 2
subject: PHIL
description: Guided research on a theme and/or problem in epistemology/metaphysics.

name: Introductory Physics - Mechanics
subject: PHYS
restrictions: Not open to students taking or having taken PHYS 131, CEGEP objective 00UR or equivalent
description: An introductory course in physics without calculus, covering mechanics (kinematics, dynamics, energy, and rotational motion), oscillations and waves, sound, light, and wave optics.

name: Introductory Physics - Electromagnetism
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHYS 101.
corequisitesText: Corequisite: MATH 139 or higher level calculus course.
restrictions: Not open to students taking or having taken PHYS 142, CEGEP objective 00UR or equivalent
description: Electric field and potential. D.C. circuits and measurements. Capacitance. Magnetic field and induction. Electromagnetic waves and geometrical optics.

name: Mechanics Laboratory (Life Sciences)
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: Lecture component of PHYS 101 or equivalent
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking PHYS 101
description: The laboratory component of PHYS 101.

name: E&M Laboratory (Life Sciences)
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: Lecture component of PHYS 102 or equivalent
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking PHYS 102
description: The laboratory component of PHYS 102.

name: Mechanics Laboratory
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: Lecture component of PHYS 131 or equivalent
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking PHYS 131
description: The laboratory component of PHYS 131.

name: E & M Laboratory
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: Lecture component of PHYS 142 or equivalent
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking PHYS 142
description: The laboratory component of PHYS 142.

name: Mechanics and Waves
subject: PHYS
corequisitesText: Corequisite: MATH 139 or higher level calculus course.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking PHYS 101, or who have taken CEGEP objective 00UR or equivalent.
description: The basic laws and principles of Newtonian mechanics; oscillations, waves, and wave optics.

name: Electromagnetism and Optics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHYS 131.
corequisitesText: Corequisite: MATH 141 or higher level calculus course.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking PHYS 102, or who have taken CEGEP objective 00US or equivalent.
description: The basic laws of electricity and magnetism; geometrical optics.

name: Space, Time and Matter
subject: PHYS
restrictions: Not open to students in a Physics program. Not open to students who have taken PHYS 200.
description: A nonmathematical, conceptual look at physics, beginning with the idea of space and time, continuing with the historical development of Newtonian mechanics of celestial motion, electricity and magnetism, ether and light, Einstein's special and general theories of relativity, quantum mechanics, matter and antimatter, cosmology and the big bang.

name: Everyday Physics
subject: PHYS
description: The day-to-day physics behind the materials and phenomena around us. Demonstrations of the intriguing properties of materials and the simple physical theories explaining them.

name: Our Evolving Universe
subject: PHYS
restrictions: Not open to students in a Physics program. Not open to students who have taken PHYS 204 or PHYS 205.
description: An elementary course on astronomy and astrophysics. Positional astronomy and finding your way about the sky. Our evolving picture of the universe. Properties and origins of the solar system. The Big Bang and modern cosmology.

name: The Milky Way Inside and Out
subject: PHYS
restrictions: Not open to students in a Physics program. Not open to students who have taken PHYS 204 or PHYS 206.
description: An elementary course on astronomy. Star origins and star formation, supernovae, white dwarfs, neutron stars, and black holes. Galaxies, their structure and their interactions. Stellar clusters, the interstellar medium. Galactic classification and galaxy evolution.

name: Astrobiology
subject: PHYS
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking EPSC 182, ANAT 182, or EPSC 186.
description: Astrobiology is the study of life throughout the universe. The course will cover the search for, and characterization of, habitable worlds. We will explore the formation and evolution of stars and planets, the astronomical and geological factors that impact a planet's habitability, the evolution of life on Earth, and the potential for biological evolution beyond an organism's planet of origin.

name: Physics of Music
subject: PHYS
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHYS 225
description: An introduction to the physics of music. Properties of sound and their perception as pitch, loudness, and timbre. Dissonance, consonance, and musical intervals and tuning. Physics of sound propagation and reflection. Resonance. Acoustic properties of pipes, strings, bars, and membranes, and sound production in wind, string, and percussion instruments. The human voice. Room reverberation and acoustics. Directional characteristics of sound sources.

name: Energy and the Environment
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: CEGEP Physics or PHYS 102 or PHYS 142 or permission of the instructor
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken PHYS 184.
description: Energy fundamentals, generation of electricity, heat engines, fossil fuel production and consumption, local and global effects, economic impact, transportation, and pollution and environmental impact of energy use. Non-renewable energy sources (fossil fuels, nuclear) and renewable sources (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal).

name: Dynamics of Simple Systems
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: CEGEP Physics or PHYS 131.
restrictions: Not open to students taking or having passed PHYS 251
description: Translational motion under Newton's laws; forces, momentum, work/energy theorem. Special relativity; Lorentz transforms, relativistic mechanics, mass/energy equivalence. Topics in rotational dynamics. Noninertial frames.

name: Heat and Waves
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: CEGEP Physics or PHYS 142, and CEGEP chemistry or CHEM 120, and PHYS 230.
restrictions: Not open to students taking or having passed PHYS 253
description: The laws of thermodynamics and their consequences. Thermodynamics of P-V-T systems and simple heat engines. Free, driven, and damped harmonic oscillators. Coupled systems and normal modes. Fourier methods. Wave motion and dispersion. The wave equation.

name: Signal Processing
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: CEGEP physics or PHYS 142.
description: Linear circuit elements, resonance, network theorems, diodes, transistors, amplifiers, feedback, integrated circuits.

name: Electricity and Magnetism
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: CEGEP Physics, MATH 222
description: Properties of electromagnetic fields, dipole and quadropole fields and their interactions, chemical binding of molecules, electromagnetic properties of materials, Maxwell's equations and properties of electromagnetic waves, propagation of waves in media.

name: Honours Classical Mechanics 1
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: CEGEP physics or PHYS 131.
restrictions: Not open to students taking or having taken PHYS 230.
description: Newton's laws, work energy, angular momentum. Harmonic oscillator, forced oscillations. Inertial forces, rotating frames. Central forces, centre of mass, planetary orbits, Kepler's laws.

name: Thermal Physics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: CEGEP physics or PHYS 131, and CEGEP chemistry or CHEM 120.
restrictions: Not open to students taking or having taken PHYS 232.
description: Energy, work, heat; first law. Temperature, entropy; second law. Absolute zero; third law. Equilibrium, equations of state, gases, liquids, solids, magnets; phase transitions.

name: Experimental Methods 1
subject: PHYS
description: Introductory laboratory work and data analysis as related to mechanics, optics and thermodynamics. Introduction to computers as they are employed for laboratory work, for data analysis and for numerical computation. Previous experience with computers is an asset, but is not required.

name: Experimental Methods 2
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHYS 257
description: Advanced laboratory work and data analysis as related to mechanics, optics and thermodynamics. Computers will be employed routinely for data analysis and for numerical computation, and, particularly, to facilitate the use of Fourier methods.

name: Modern Physics and Relativity
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: CEGEP physics or PHYS 142.
description: History of special relativity; Lorentz transformations: kinematics and dynamics; transformation of electric and magnetic forces; introduction to topics in modern physics.

name: Introduction to Biophysics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: BIOL 200 or BIOL 219; MATH 222; PHYS 230 and (PHYS 232 or PHYS 253), or permission of the instructor.
restrictions: Not open to students who have taken or are taking BIOL 319
description: Emerging physical approaches and quantitative measurement techniques are providing new insights into longstanding biological questions. This course will present underlying physical theory, quantitative measurement techniques, and significant findings in molecular and cellular biophysics. Principles covered include Brownian motion, low Reynolds-number environments, forces relevant to cells and molecules, chemical potentials, and free energies. These principles are applied to enzymes as molecular machines, membranes, DNA, and RNA.

name:
Introductory
 Astrophysics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: MATH 222; PHYS 230 or PHYS 260 or permission of the instructor.
description: A survey of astrophysics ranging from stars and planets, to compact objects, galaxies, and the large-scale evolution of the Universe. A calculusbased course, with a focus on simple mathematical derivations that capture the essential physics.

name: Data Science and Observational Astrophysics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): COMP 208; PHYS 257
description:
Data analysis methods as applied in experimental physics, with an emphasis on applications in observational astrophysics. An introduction to Bayesian
inference, model selection, Markov Chain Monte Carlo, common probability distributions, jackknives and null tests, as they are used in the analysis of observational data from across the electromagnetic spectrum.

name: Electronics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHYS 241 or permission of instructor
description: Semiconductor devices, basic transistor circuits, operational amplifiers, combinatorial and sequential logic, integrated circuits, analogue to digital converters. The laboratory component covers design, construction and testing of basic electronic circuits.

name: Statistical Physics with Biophysical Applications
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite(s): [BIOL 219 and (PHYS 253 or PHYS 232 or CHEM 345)] or permission of the instructor
restrictions: Not opened to students having taken PHYS 333, PHYS 362, or CHEM 365
description: This interdisciplinary course introduces Statistical Physics illustrated with modern biophysical applications. Principles covered include partition functions, Boltzmann distribution, bosons, fermions, Bose Einstein condensates, Ferni gases, chemical potential, thermodynamical forces, biochemical kinetics, and an introduction to noise and phase transitions in biology.

name: Topics in Classical Mechanics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHYS 230
restrictions: Not open to students having passed PHYS 451 or PHYS 351
description: Forced and damped oscillators, Newtonian mechanics in three dimensions, rotational motion, Lagrangian and Hamiltonian mechanics, small vibrations, normal modes. Nonlinear dynamics and chaos.

name: Thermal and Statistical Physics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHYS 232
restrictions: Not open to students taking or having passed PHYS 362
description: Introductory equilibrium statistical mechanics. Quantum states, probabilities, ensemble averages. Entropy, temperature, Boltzmann factor, chemical potential. Photons and phonons. Fermi-Dirac and Bose-Einstein distributions; applications.

name: Measurements Laboratory in General Physics
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisite: PHYS 241 or permission of instructor
description: Introduction to modern techniques of measurement. The use of computers in performing and analysing experiments. Data reduction, statistical methods, report writing. Extensive use of computers is made in this laboratory; therefore some familiarity with computers and computing is an advantage.

name: Majors Electricity and Magnetism
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: CEGEP physics or PHYS 142, MATH 222
restrictions: Not open to students who have passed PHYS 242 or PHYS 350
description: The electrostatic field and scalar potential. Dielectric properties of matter. Energy in the electrostatic field. Methods for solving problems in electrostatics. The magnetic field. Induction and inductance. Energy in the magnetic field. Magnetic properties of matter. Maxwell's equations. The dipole approximation.

name: Majors Electromagnetic Waves
subject: PHYS
prerequisitesText: Prerequisites: PHYS 340 or PHYS 242, Mathematics MATH 314, MATH 315
